skill.name: routeros-mndp
skill.description: Reference guide for MikroTik Neighbor Discovery Protocol, including MNDP packet layout, RouterOS /ip/neighbor behavior, discovery timing, parsing notes, and safe interface configuration.
skill.summary: Use MNDP details to discover, parse, troubleshoot, and secure MikroTik RouterOS neighbor discovery.
skill.author: tikoci

security_audit.summary: Static external command and weak cryptography findings are false positives caused by Markdown code formatting, RouterOS command examples, and protocol names. The skill is documentation-only, but it teaches unauthenticated LAN discovery and metadata collection through MNDP, so publication should include a network-use warning.

medium_findings.title: Unauthenticated LAN Discovery Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill explains how MNDP uses UDP broadcast and multicast to discover RouterOS devices and expose identity, version, board, IP, MAC, and uptime data. This is legitimate network administration content, but it can support reconnaissance on a local broadcast domain if used without authorization.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network behavior and exposed metadata are explicitly documented in the skill. The content also includes defensive guidance, so the concern is dual-use rather than confirmed malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Formatting Misidentified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged many backtick-delimited Markdown terms and fenced examples as Ruby shell backtick execution. No Ruby code or automatic execution path is present in the skill file; the examples are documentation for RouterOS CLI, shell, TypeScript, and C usage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review shows Markdown inline code and fenced documentation examples, not executable Ruby backticks. The repository contains a single SKILL.md file with no script entry point.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Flags Are Protocol Text False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography findings point to MNDP and IPv6 multicast references, not to cryptographic functions or deprecated algorithms. No hashing, encryption, or credential handling logic appears in the reviewed file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are descriptive protocol text and multicast addresses. There is no evidence of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or other weak cryptographic implementation.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Warnings Are Presented as Security Context
low_findings.description: The static reconnaissance findings identify text about device identity, addresses, and metadata exposure. The skill frames these facts as expected MNDP behavior and includes advice to disable discovery on untrusted interfaces.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The same lines that describe discoverable metadata also provide defensive context. This supports legitimate administration and does not show malicious targeting or exfiltration intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: UDP Broadcast and Multicast Discovery Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes examples for sending or listening to MNDP discovery traffic on UDP port 5678. This network behavior should be limited to authorized local networks because MNDP is unauthenticated.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code and protocol notes clearly describe UDP discovery traffic. The pattern is expected for MNDP tools but carries dual-use network scanning risk.

content.value_statement: MikroTik neighbor discovery can be hard to parse when devices appear on several interfaces or respond slowly. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical MNDP protocol details for discovery, parsing, troubleshooting, and hardening.
actual_capabilities: Explains MNDP transport, packet structure, TLV fields, byte order, and common response values.
Maps MNDP behavior to RouterOS /ip/neighbor and WinBox neighbor discovery workflows.
Guides parser design for identity, version, board, interface, IPv4, IPv6, MAC, and uptime fields.
Describes multi-interface behavior so duplicate-looking router entries can be grouped correctly.
Provides timing, retry, socket, and port-sharing notes for reliable local discovery tools.
Highlights hardening steps for limiting neighbor discovery on untrusted RouterOS interfaces.
limitations: The skill is a protocol reference, not a ready-made scanner or RouterOS client.
MNDP only works inside the local Layer 2 broadcast domain unless a tunnel forwards traffic.
Results can be incomplete when scan windows are short or local networks drop broadcast traffic.
The skill does not authenticate devices or verify that discovered metadata is trustworthy.

use_cases.title: Troubleshoot Missing WinBox Neighbors
use_cases.description: Diagnose why a RouterOS device does not appear in neighbor lists and decide whether timing, interface settings, or broadcast scope is the likely cause.

use_cases.title: Build an MNDP Parser
use_cases.description: Use the TLV field reference and byte-order notes to parse RouterOS discovery packets into useful device records.

use_cases.title: Review Discovery Exposure
use_cases.description: Identify what MNDP reveals on a local network and plan RouterOS settings that reduce exposure on guest or public interfaces.

prompt_templates.title: Explain MNDP Basics
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain how MikroTik Neighbor Discovery Protocol works on a local network. Include what RouterOS devices send, what WinBox sees, and why MNDP does not cross routers.

prompt_templates.title: Troubleshoot Discovery Results
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me troubleshoot why a RouterOS device is missing from /ip/neighbor and WinBox neighbors. Consider interface discovery settings, scan timing, duplicate identities, and broadcast domain limits.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Packet Parser
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an MNDP response parser for a discovery tool. Explain the TLV fields to extract, byte order rules, malformed packet handling, and grouping logic for multi-interface routers.

prompt_templates.title: Harden Discovery Safely
prompt_templates.prompt: Review an MNDP discovery implementation and RouterOS configuration for security risks. Recommend how to limit discovery to trusted interfaces while preserving useful operations.

output_examples.input: Why does one MikroTik router show as several neighbors?
output_examples.output: The router sends one MNDP announcement per active interface. Group entries by identity, then use interface name, MAC address, and IP address to distinguish each interface.

output_examples.input: What should my MNDP parser extract first?
output: Identity and interface name for display.
MAC address and IP address for uniqueness.
Version, board, and uptime for inventory and troubleshooting.
Both board field variants when present.

output_examples.input: How can I reduce MNDP exposure on a guest network?
output_examples.output: Disable neighbor discovery on untrusted interfaces and keep MNDP enabled only on trusted management networks. Review RouterOS discovery settings after interface changes.

best_practices: Use MNDP only on networks where you have authorization to discover devices.
Send multiple refresh packets during a scan window before treating missing devices as unreachable.
Group neighbor entries by identity and confirm with MAC, interface, and IP data before taking action.
anti_patterns: Do not run broadcast discovery on public, guest, or third-party networks without permission.
Do not assume a missing MNDP response means a router is offline or unreachable.
Do not expose RouterOS neighbor discovery on interfaces that do not need local administration.

faq.question: Does this skill run network scans?
faq.answer: No. It provides protocol guidance and examples. Any discovery tool must be built or run separately by the user.

faq.question: Which port does MNDP use?
faq.answer: MNDP uses UDP port 5678 for sending and receiving discovery traffic on the local network.

faq.question: Why can one router appear multiple times?
faq.answer: RouterOS sends separate announcements for active interfaces. Each entry can have a different MAC address, interface name, or IP address.

faq.question: Is MNDP authenticated?
faq.answer: No. MNDP is unauthenticated and reveals device metadata to systems on the same broadcast domain.

faq.question: Can MNDP discover routers across the internet?
faq.answer: No. MNDP is intended for the local Layer 2 network and normally does not cross routers.

faq.question: Can I use this with Claude Code or Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill is written as reference material for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.
